rulings.Case Title: SN SANDS CORPORATION, DBA S&S TRUCKING, VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, ACTING BY AND ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 2, Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction. Plaintiff and Petitioner SN Sands Corporation, dba S & S Trucking's application for a preliminary injunction is denied. Petitioner provided certain biosolids and grit hauling services to Defendant and Respondent City and County of San Francisco under a contract that, as extended, expired on September 30, 2019. (Magante Decl. 2, 18 & Ex. L.) In April 2019, a replacement contract for the same services was the subject of an Invitation for Bids issued by the City. (Id. 2.) Although Petitioner submitted a bid, a competitor, Denali Water Solutions, was the low bidder for Aggregate 1, and accordingly on June 12, 2019, the City issued a notice of intent to award that portion of the contract to Denali. (Id. 9 & Ex. F.) (Petitioner apparently was awarded another portion of the contract, for Aggregate 2.) Petitioner's subsequent bid protest was rejected by the City on July 15, 2019. (Id. 11-13 & Exs. G-I.) On September 25, 2019, the City issued a contract to Denali, which signed it on September 26, 2019. (Id. 14 & Ex. J.) On September 27, 2019, the Court denied Petitioner's request for a TRO and set a hearing on an OSC re preliminary injunction. [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 4, Plaintiff,Cross Defendant Jones And Devoys' Motion For Terminating Sanctions, Or In The Alternative, Evidentiary Sanctions And Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $90 Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. Counsel to moving party shall submit a printed proposed order to Department 301 before the hearing with a copy in Word format via email to jameshfleming@gmail.com. Unless contested, no appearance is required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jamesfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MITCHELL ENGINEERING/OBAYASHI CORPORATION ET AL VS. ST. PAUL FIRE & MARINE INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Pursuant To Ccp Sections 583.410 And 583.420
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 5, # 2 - DEFENDANT WOODRUFF-SAWYER & CO.'s Motion To Dismiss Pursuant To CCP Sections 583.410 And 583.420. The Court has the discretion to dismiss an action for delay in prosecution if (1) service is not made within two years after the action is commenced against the defendant or (2) the action is not brought to trial within three years after the action is commenced against the defendant. (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 583.410, 583.420(a)(1),(2)(A).) Both grounds apply here, and Plaintiffs have failed to make a showing of excusable delay sufficient to justify their delay. [The Court's full tentative rulings on the 3 motions were emailed to the parties.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Woodruff-Sawyer is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREA L RICHARDSON VS. UCSF MEDICAL CENTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant The Regents of the University of California's demurrer to the first and second causes of action of Plaintiff Andrea Richardson's second amended complaint is sustained. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 430.10(e).) Although Plaintiff did not file and serve her opposition at least 9 court days prior to the hearing, as required, and the Court previously warned Plaintiff that failure to comply with the rules could be grounds for sustaining a demurrer without leave to amend and dismissing the action, the Court decides the demurrer on its merits. The demurrer as to the first cause of action for breach of contract is sustained without leave to amend because the complaint fails to establish the existence of a written contract between the parties. Plaintiff alleges the parties entered into a written contract, but in her opposition she argues instead that "the contract between Defendant was specifically through an implied patient-doctor relationship which would not be required to be in writing." She also refers to medical consent forms, but has not attached them to the second amended complaint or alleged their contents. A doctor's liability under a breach of contract theory is limited. "[A] doctor may be held liable on a theory of breach of contract where he has clearly and unequivocally warranted that a course of treatment recommended by him will, inevitably, produce a certain result." (Pulvers v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (1979) 99 Cal.App.3d 560, 564-565; accord, McKinney v. Nash (1981) 120 Cal.App.3d 428, 442 ["To recover for breach of warranty or contract in a medical malpractice case, there must be proof of an express contract by which the physician clearly promises a particular result and the patient consents to treatment in reliance on that promise. [Citation.] The promise of a particular result is to be distinguished from the mere generalized statement that the result of treatment will be good. [Citation.]]) No such express contract or promise is alleged in the second amended complaint, and plaintiff's allegations regarding what, "as implied," she "understood and expected" defendant to do make it clear that she does not claim any such express contract was entered into. The demurrer as to the second cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is sustained without leave to amend. The complaint does not allege that Defendant made a false representation as to a past or existing material fact. (See Majd v. Bank of America, N.A. (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 1293, 1307.) Implied assertions or representations are not enough. (Wilson v. Century 21 Great Western Realty (1993) 15 Cal.App.4th 298, 306 ["Negligent misrepresentation is a species of fraud or deceit specifically requiring a "positive assertion" or "assertion" of fact."]) In her opposition, Plaintiff argues she asked a transplant clinical coordinator before her surgery what would happen if there were complications, and that the coordinator responded, "we'll take care of it." But, to be actionable, a negligent misrepresentation ordinarily must be as to past or existing material facts. (Tarmann v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. (1991) 2 Cal.App.4th 153, 158 ["[P]redictions as to future events, or statements as to future action by some third party, are deemed opinions, and not actionable fraud."].) [Continued to Part 2 of 2] =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. BIJOU-CENTURY, LLC (DBA NEW CENTURY THEATRE) ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Strike First Amended Complaint And References To John Doe Therein; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 8, 2-DEFENDANT BIJOU-CENTURY, LLC Motion To Strike First Amended Complaint And References To John Doe Therein. Defendant Bijou-Century, LLC's motion to strike is denied and plaintiff is granted leave of court to proceed anonymously. "The judicial use of 'Doe plaintiffs' to protect legitimate privacy rights has gained wide currency, particularly given the rapidity and ubiquity of disclosures over the World Wide Web." (Starbucks Corp. v. Superior Court (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1436, 1452 fn. 7); see, e.g., Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531 [former Boy Scouts sued under pseudonym based on allegations that city police officer sexually assaulted them while they were teenagers]; Doe v. Superior Court (Luster) (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 750, 754 [rape victim permitted to file complaint under fictitious name in order to preserve her anonymity].) Federal courts allow parties to use pseudonyms "in special circumstances when the party's need for anonymity outweighs prejudice to the opposing party and the public's interest in knowing the party's identity." (Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir. 2000) 214 F.3d 1058, 1068.) "Applying this balancing test, courts have permitted plaintiffs to use pseudonyms in three situations: (1) when identification creates a risk of retaliatory physical or mental harm, [citations]; (2) when anonymity is necessary 'to preserve privacy in a matter of sensitive and highly personal nature,' [citations] and (3) when the anonymous party is 'compelled to admit [his or her] intention to engage in illegal conduct, thereby risking criminal prosecution.'" (Id.; see also Doe v. Lincoln Unified School Dist. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 758, 767.) This case presents a clear example of the need to conceal a party's identity to preserve his privacy in a matter of a highly personal nature. Plaintiff alleges that he was involuntarily drugged, kidnapped, stripped naked, and taken advantage of financially. (Amended Complaint, 37, 40, 43, 65-68.) Such allegations open plaintiff up to public ridicule and humiliation and the public has little to no interest in knowing plaintiff's identity. (Doe Decl. 9.) Defendant's claim of prejudice is spurious as plaintiff has already disclosed his identity for purposes of investigation, and has also offered to cooperate in discovery so as to minimize any impact of his proceeding anonymously. (Acikalin Decl., 6-7, 9, 10.) The only legitimate argument that defendant makes in its motion to strike is that plaintiff should have obtained a court order before proceeding anonymously. As Judge Ulmer previously noted, such an order is necessary. (See 10/9/19 order denying motion to compel arbitration without prejudice). The Court now grants plaintiff permission to proceed anonymously. Even though the Court concludes that defendant has no legitimate basis for its motion to strike, the Court denies plaintiff's request for sanctions. As noted, plaintiff should have obtained an order allowing anonymity concurrently when he filed his complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. BIJOU-CENTURY, LLC (DBA NEW CENTURY THEATRE) ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 8, 1-DEFENDANT BIJOU-CENTURY, LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Bijou-Century, LLC's demurrer to the first cause of action for sexual battery in violation of Civil Code, § 1708.5 is overruled. The amended complaint alleges sufficient ultimate facts showing that plaintiff was a victim of sexual battery and defendant is vicariously liable. (Amended Complaint, 16, 21, 23, 37, 38, 40, 41, 42, 44, 49, 63, 65, 68, 90, 94, 95, 96, 97, 99, 108, 109, 11, 114.) A factual issue remains whether plaintiff was sexually battered. Whether plaintiff lacks memory of the specifics of the incident is not dispositive, since it is conceivable that other evidence could support the claim. The allegation that plaintiff's roommate found plaintiff stripped naked and in the presence of half-naked strangers supports the claim. (Amended Complaint, 65.) Defendant also raises a factual issue regarding vicarious liability. The amended complaint is littered with allegations that the co-defendants were acting at defendant's behest, whether as employees, agents, or otherwise. The Court further notes that defendant allegedly benefited financially from the transactions. (Amended Complaint, 41, 99.) Lastly, defendant's factual argument that plaintiff cannot establish vicarious liability or that the co-defendants were not acting within the course and scope of their employment is based on cases decided on summary judgment, not demurrer. (See Lisa M. v Henry Mayo Newhall Memorial Hospital (1995) 12 Cal.4th 291; Farmers Insurance Group v. County of Santa Clara (1995) 11 Cal.4th 992; Z.V. v. County of Riverside (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 889; Montague v. AMN Healthcare, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th1515; Yamaguchi v. Harnsmut (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 472.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: YI LI ET AL VS. KINDRED NURSING CENTERS WEST, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 11, 1-DEFENDANT JOHN PIERCE's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant John W. Pierce, M.D.'s demurrer is overruled. The demurrer to the first cause of action for negligence/willful misconduct is overruled. The claim does not need to be segregated into two causes of action because it is a single tort. (See Berkley v. Dowds (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 518, 526 ["[Willful misconduct] is not a separate tort, but simply an aggravated form of negligence" (internal quotations omitted)]; Doe v. United States Youth Soccer Assn., Inc. (2017) 8 Cal.App.5th 1118, 1140 [same].) For willful misconduct, plaintiff must allege (1) actual or constructive knowledge of the peril to be apprehended, (2) actual or constructive knowledge that injury is probable, as opposed to a possible, result of the danger, and (3) conscious failure to act to avoid the peril. (Id.) Here, plaintiff's allegations support the claim. (Complaint, 30-47.) The demurrer to the second cause of action for elder abuse is overruled. The above adduced allegations support the elder abuse cause of action. "Abuse of an elder" includes "Physical abuse, neglect, abandonment, isolation, abduction, or other treatment with resulting physical harm or pain or mental suffering" and "The deprivation by a care custodian of goods or services that are necessary to avoid physical harm or mental suffering." (Welf. & Inst. Code, §15610.07(a)(1),(2); see also Welf. & Inst. Code, § 15610.57(b)(2) [defining "neglect" to include "Failure to provide medical care for physical and mental needs."].) In this case, plaintiff alleges that decedent's low sodium levels and condition showed that decedent needed to be transferred back to the hospital immediately and defendant recklessly or maliciously withheld that care. (Compl. 30-31, 33-34.) A doctor that opposes hospitalization over several days where circumstances indicate it is medically necessary could be liable for neglect. (Mack v. Soung (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 966, 973, disapproved on other grounds, Winn v. Pioneer Medical Group, Inc. (2016) 63 Cal.4th 148 ["We have no trouble concluding that a doctor who . . . opposes [a patient's] hospitalization where circumstances indicate it is medically necessary . . . commits neglect within the meaning of the [Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act"]; see also, e.g., Sababin v. Superior Court (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 81, 89-90 [reversing summary adjudication on dependent adult abuse cause of action where there were triable issues as to whether rehabilitation center's employees were guilty of reckless, oppressive or malicious neglect because there was evidence that they failed to follow plaintiff's care plan, thereby withholding necessary medical care].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: YI LI ET AL VS. KINDRED NURSING CENTERS WEST, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 11, 2-DEFENDANT JOHN PIERCE's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant John W. Pierce, M.D.'s motion to strike plaintiff's request for punitive damages is granted as to Paragraph 64 of the complaint but is otherwise denied as to the claim for punitive damages in the prayer for relief in the complaint. Plaintiff did not comply with the prefiling requirement of Code of Civil Procedure, § 425.13. However, such compliance is not necessary where a party pleads a viable elder abuse claim. (See Covenant Care, Inc. v. Superior Court (2004) 32 Cal.4th 771, 790 ["Where the gravamen of an action is violation of the Elder Abuse Act, Central Pathology's rationale for applying section 425.13 to the common law intentional torts at issue in that case does not obtain."].) The motion to strike the claim for attorney's fees is denied because such relief can be available to the prevailing party on an elder abuse claim. (See Welf. & Insts. Code, § 15657(a).) The motion to strike is granted without leave to amend as to 44-47. Those allegations relate to Medical Board disciplinary proceedings against Dr. Pierce concerning his treatment of other patients, and the Court strikes them as "improper" or "irrelevant" per Code of Civil Procedure, § 436(a). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KEESUNG NAM VS. STEPHEN MARK MOSKOWITZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 12, # 1 - DEFENDANTS STEPHEN MOSKOWITZ, MOSKOWITZ, LLP, MOSKOWITZ LAW CORPORATION DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrer to Plaintiff's complaint as to the second cause of action for violation of Civil Code § 1632 is overruled. The California Translation Act provides that any person engaged in a trade or business who negotiates primarily in certain specified foreign languages including Korean, "orally or in writing in the course of entering into any of the following, shall deliver to the other party to the contract or agreement and prior to the execution thereof a translation of the contract or agreement in the language in which the contract or agreement was negotiatied, that includes a translation of every term and condition in that contract or agreement." (Civ. Code § 1632(b).) The Act specifically applies to attorneys' fees agreements. (§ 1632(b)(6).) Plaintiff alleges he speaks Korean, that Defendants knew this and therefore provided a Korean translator during their negotiations. (2d. Amend. Compl. 12-13, 19-21, 39-40.) Plaintiff alleges the terms were orally negotiated in Korean and formalized in signed written agreements. (Id. at 39-41.) These written agreements were in English only; Defendants never provided Plaintiff with a Korean translation, thereby violating the California Translation Act. (Id.) If an attorney fails to provide the translation required by statute, the client may rescind the contract. (Civ. Code, § 1632(k) ["Upon a failure to comply with the provisions of this section, the person aggrieved may rescind the contract or agreement in the manner provided by this chapter."]; California Practice Guide: Professional Responsibility (The Rutter Guide 2019) 5:619 [same].) Plaintiff further alleges Defendants took no action on Plaintiff's behalf regarding the second fee agreement from 2016, and that three days after confirming the receipt of the $200,000 flat fee, Defendants informed Plaintiff that he did not qualify for the treaty "tiebreaker" position. (2d. Amend. Compl. 27-28.) Plaintiff also alleges Defendants did not complete certain unfinished services under the first fee agreement. (Id. at 30.) [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES ET AL VS. OLIVIER AUFFRET ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Default And Default Judgment Of Or As To Defendants Olivier Auffret And Virginie Leclair
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 13, 1-DEFENDANT OLIVIER AUFFRET, VIRGINIE LECLAIR's Motion And Motion To Vacate Default And Default Judgment Of Or As To Defendants Olivier Auffret And Virginie Leclair. Defendants' motion to vacate the default and default judgment taken against them on August 27 and September 6, 2019 is granted. A defendant may seek discretionary relief from default on grounds of "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." (Code Civ. Proc., § 473(b).) "Section 473 is often applied liberally where the party in default moves promptly to seek relief, and the party opposing the motion will not suffer prejudice if relief is granted." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 233.) Defendants set forth facts showing excusable neglect, entitling them to relief. First, "[t]o entitle a party to relief [for excusable neglect] the acts which brought about the default must have been the acts of a reasonably prudent person under the same circumstances." (Jackson v. Bank of America (1983) 141 Cal.App.3d 55, 58.) Defendants, French residents and citizens, show that following service, they were reasonably diligent in contacting their French counsel and seeking to retain an attorney in the US, that upon receiving a retainer agreement they promptly signed it and sent it back, and that it took some time to pay counsel's fees because of banking and holiday delays. (Auffret/LeClair Decl. 3-19.) Second, an application for discretionary relief shall be made within a reasonable time, in no case exceeding six months." (Code Civ. Proc. § 473(b).) This motion was made within a reasonable time. Defendants filed this motion on October 7, about a month after default judgment was entered. Third, a motion under § 473 is granted liberally if the party opposing the motion will not suffer prejudice if relief is granted. (Elston, 38 Cal.3d at 233.) Plaintiffs' argument that if relief is granted they would be "required to spend much time and incur large expenses to defend themselves" is insufficient to show prejudice. (See Plfs.' Opp. to Mot. to Vacate at 5; Rogalski v. Nabers Cadillac (1992) 11 Cal.App.4th 816, 822 ["The only true prejudice to [plaintiff] is that he will now have to go back and try the case on the merits. But since the law strongly favors trial and disposition on the merits, there is no prejudice."].) Finally, Defendants sufficiently support their motion for relief. "Case law . . . requires the party seeking relief to accompany his notice of motion for relief with an affidavit of merits or verified pleading which sets up a valid defense." (Transit Ads v. Tanner Motor Livery (1969) 270 Cal.App.2d 275, 281.) Defendants include with their motion a general denial with several defenses to the complaint, and thus meet their requirements for relief under CCP § 473(b). (Defs.' RJN ISO Mot. to Vacate, Ex. 12.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES ET AL VS. OLIVIER AUFFRET ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion Of Plaintiffs Luscutoff, Lendormy And Associates And Jean-Yves Lendormy For Order Directing The Filing Of An Undertaking [Ccp Sec. 1030]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 13, 2-PLAINTIFF LUSCUTOFF, LENDORMY & ASSOCIATES, JEAN-YVES LENDORMY's Motion For Order Directing The Filing Of An Undertaking [Ccp Sec. 1030]. Plaintiffs' motion for an order directing the filing of an undertaking pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1030 is denied. Section 1030 provides: "When the plaintiff in an action or special proceeding resides out of the state, or is a foreign corporation, the defendant may at any time apply to the court by noticed motion for an order requiring the plaintiff to file an undertaking to secure an award of costs and attorney's fees." Plaintiffs argue under Code of Civil Procedure § 387, a plaintiff includes a cross-complainant. Plaintiffs are wrong. Yao v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 327 squarely rejected this exact argument, holding that a California plaintiff who sues an out-of-state defendant cannot move the court to require the defendant/cross-complainant to post security in order to prosecute the cross-complaint, for two reasons: "The first is that section 1030 unambiguously refers only to a 'plaintiff'; when the Legislature intends for a specific statutory provision to apply to both a plaintiff and cross-complainant, it expressly indicates that intent. The second is that to construe the statute to include an out-of-state cross-complainant would not promote the policy served by the statute." 'that "plaintiff could not move the trial court to require defendant to post security under Code Civ. Proc., § 1030, in order to prosecute the cross-complaint. Section 1030 unambiguously refers only to a 'plaintiff,' and when the Legislature intends for a specific statutory provision to apply to both a plaintiff and a cross-complainant, it expressly indicates that intent." (Id. at 331-332.) Contrary to Plaintiffs' unsupported argument on reply, Yao is binding on this Court. (Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: 667, L.P. ET AL VS. OMNIOX, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Exhibit A To Declaration Of Michael Dybbs In Support Of Plaintiffs Application For Right To Attach Order And Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 30, 2019, Line 16, 1-DEFENDANT OMNIOX, INC.'s Motion To Seal Exhibit A To Declaration Of Michael Dybbs In Support Of Plaintiffs Application For Right To Attach Order And Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment. Granted. No opposition filed and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a revised proposed order which recites the above findings and states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
